author: @simonchampa9961
text:
The pelton wheel utilizes a circular jet of fluid.  That's why the cup has a double curvature.  

The wind turbine is fed from a line source.  It isn't difficult to understand why double curvature doesn't help.

Also water and air have very different properties.  You can't assume that because they are both fluids that the same geometry will be optimal.  

FYI a pelton wheel is only the most efficient water turbine on low specific speed applications.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, the idea of using a cup shape to redirect wind on the outside via the Coanda effect rather than merely catch it like a scoop is what we see in the Ugrinsky turbine. The channel in the middle of the Ugrinsky turbine lets the wind through,cand it follows the contour of the smaller radius curve, deflecting it and adding momentum to the turbine.

author: @victoryfirst2878
text:
I have to respond to your comments and views Sir Robert. One of the biggest reasons that industry does not use the latest technology is that there are a boatload of old turbines still available made by G E, Westinghouse, and others made during WW2 that are mothballed and new old stock. They are slowly purchased for the almighty greenbacks. I know for a fact that what you showed is most certainly higher efficiency units for sure. I would bet today with the crazy prices for energy that will most certainly change fella. 
Nice informative video for sure Sir. Peace vf

author: @taranof
text:
Double cups will probably not help as the wind is not directed at the center ridge like the water jet is.
The main thing is the airfoil effect that creates lift and makes this rotor supposedly more efficient than a mere resistance runner like the Savonius.
Please look at Lenz and Lenz 2 rotors as they are developments of the C rotor.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: The Lenz 2 was a big improvement.  The c-rotor has great starting torque but looses it quickly as it starts spinning.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: see the images at time stamp 2:18 to 2:23 - The simplest rotor of this type is at time stamp 1:52 to 1:55 - it's usually known as the filippini rotor invented in the 70's i believe - refer to this paper - Design and Performance Evaluation of an Economical Vertical Axis Wind Turbine - for an analysis of the filippini rotor inserted into a savonius design made from half barrels -  36% improvement was noted - so I think you might not be right there mate - sorry

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the lens 2 was taken from of the patent or at least the patent i showed already covered it - the patent was issued in 1991

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you for the reference.  I requested a copy from ReasearchGate.  I keep looking for VAWT rotors that I haven't tested yet.  2.2 TSR is the best I've tested for "drag" type turbines.  I only test on a very small scale so I do not test any "lift" type turbines.

author: @scottbruce5376
text: Improvement Idea: I would be inclined to use a flexible membrane for the cup so that when it comes around the other way, it easily collapses into a more aerodynamic shape and creates less resistance.

replies.author: @scottbruce5376
replies.text: I also have a suspicion that at various speeds, a different cup depth would be helpful.  Perhaps having these spring load so they roll out more or less "sail" when the wind pressure increases might greatly increase efficiency.

author: @miniac60
text: The pelton wheel has a heavy liquid as the motive force moving through air. This version suffers from greater relative drag because the motive force and the drag force are the same fluids.

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: Ye that's it, uses a high density fluid surrounding by a low density fluid that doesn't impinge much on the performance, while a pelton wheel in air, used a low density fluid surrounded by the same fluid, so it difficult to design a bucket that's not heavily affected by drag

replies.author: @crankfwd8209
replies.text:
This comment just opened a can of worms for me, in a good way. I am working on a vertical turbine which is a cylinder with three sails that fit flush to the body of the cylinder. There are strings wrapped round the tube near the top and the bottom. So when the wind blows a sail open, the strings pull the previous sail shut so that it is flush to the cylinder and creates less drag of the other side, going into the wind. When I tried it outside (with my pringles tube prototype😁), I noticed a phut phut noise from the sails that are forced shut, so I think the air was pushed out helping the cylinder spin but now I'm wondering if the pushed out air was creating lower pressure in front of the next "opened" sail.
Robert, if you are reading this, have you ever done a video on building a homemade wind tunnel?
Then I started thinking about sail boats. If air is blown upwards, in front of a sail, would this create lower pressure and help the sail work better? If so, could you have a yacht with a venturi tunnel inside it, so that wind collected at the back, is sped up and blown upwards at a suitable distance in front of the sail?

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: @crankfwd8209 I look at a venturi abit like a heat pump, it concentrates the pressure but with little flow, if you allow a larger flow the pressure will collapse

replies.author: @crankfwd8209
replies.text: @jimbobarooney2861 but i'm looking for lower pressure, like on an F1 car, so wouldn't that be a good thing?

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: @crankfwd8209 sorry, correct reduced pressure, check out Jacque Cousteau's turbo sail, he used a solid bluff shaped airfoil as a sail, because of the airfoils shape (a high lift airfoil) , slots were located along the trailing edge, to prevent separation a fan sucked air through the slots and out the top of the sail, maybe a venturi here could work also as a suction device?

author: @TheAdeybob
text:
stopping the air/water that's going in, from meeting the air/water that's coming out. Deliberate turbulence to extract more energy from the initial process. Excellent  stuff.
In the pelton water setup, the water is directed in a jet..and there's only air/mechanical resistance to halt the process. The efficiency would suffer muchly if the pelton wheel was in water. I got an idea there'd be similar worries designing a wind-capture pelton.

replies.author: @ThomasTheTankedEngin
replies.text: @malcolm8564 basically all the parts of the structure of a modern turbine are destined for landfill/can't be recycled.  The amount of concrete required for the platform is staggering.  I've seen suggestions that when you account for externalities they can never repay the carbon debt for building them (though we see this argument about everything and I have no idea how to validate that).  Also, as we diversify our power generation into different types of large renewable installations we require more high voltage electrical equipment.  That equipment uses a man-made gas called SF6, almost ubiquitously, as an inert gas to, basically, stop arcing and protect the equipment and operators.  There isn't really another viable option.  In an interview I read recently, an industry expert suggested they expect 15% of that gas to leak out of the equipment they install over its lifetime.  SF6 is something like 23,500 times worse than CO2 as a greenhouse gas and lasts in the environment for something like 3,200 years.

replies.author: @malcolm8564
replies.text: @ThomasTheTankedEngin those suggestions come from the fossil fuel industries out to undermine renewables. We need massive turbines because we demand a lot of energy. All the steel and copper in the machinery and column are clearly recyclable. Concrete is only a problem because they use fossil fuels to produce cement when they could be using renewable electricity to produce the heat. There's very little material in the blades, they are mostly air.

replies.author: @thomas-bg9hh
replies.text:
@malcolm8564 I'm certainly not going to pretend I'm an expert on these matters - I find it nearly impossible to tell where information is coming from anymore with all the greenwashing, half-truths, and cherry-picked information meant to grab attention out there.  But one thing that is true is that there is A LOT of resin-coated fibreglass in those blades that are mostly air - and that's just non-recyclable.

I'll also argue that we don't "need" massive turbines.  We've collectively supported large infrastructure and having awful mega-corps provide us convenience but, in my opinion, decentralisation is easily the best way to go for just about everything.
